Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Permitting Pretrial Discovery Of Financial Information Of Defendants Kim And Judith Maxwell (Cal. Civ. Code 3295(C))
Off Calendar Sep-12-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF TODD KAPLAN's Motion For Order Permitting Pretrial Discovery Of Financial Information Of Defendants Kim And Judith Maxwell (Cal. Civ. Code 3295(C)).

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The motion is denied without prejudice. A request for financial records may be allowed by the trial judge once liability is established.

Per public records, the Maxwells purchased the property in 1998 for $2,715,000. They sold it in May 2017 for $9,200,00. According to the Maxwell declaration, they spent approximately $2,000,000 to remodel the house in about 2000. Thus, a rough approximation of the Maxwell's profit is easily calculated without the necessity, at this time, of reviewing their tax returns. Moreover, if Kaplan prevails on his fraud claim, he may be eligible for punitive damages. Such damages are based on Defendants' financial condition which are not limited to their profits on the sale of the house. Income tax returns are only one measure of financial condition.

As noted in Soto v. BorgWarner Morse TEC Inc. (2015) 239 Cal. App. 4th 165, there are two alternative discovery methods available to a plaintiff: First, the plaintiff may request that the defendant "stipulate to a process by which [the defendant] would gather documents pertaining to [its] financial condition, bring them to trial under seal and make them immediately available" in the event that the jury's findings make punitive damages available. (Jabro v. Superior Court (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 754, 756. This procedure "is a frequently used and effective means of handling the matter when a claim for punitive damages is alleged," and may be suggested or facilitated by the court. (Ibid.) Finally, a plaintiff may do nothing pretrial and instead wait until liability is established to ask the court for the order described in Civil Code section 3295, subdivision (c). (Mike Davidov Co., supra, 78 Cal.App.4th at p. 609.) This strategy may backfire, however, as it is left to the court's discretion whether to delay the proceedings by ordering the defendant to produce information that could have been obtained earlier or to forge ahead upon concluding that such an order is inappropriate under the circumstances of the case. (I-CA Enterprises, Inc. v. Palram Americas, Inc. (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 257, 284.

This court finds that Plaintiff has been diligent in seeking financial records, and hereby advises the trial court that should the circumstances so indicate, Plaintiff should be allowed to discover Defendants' financial records. This court also suggests, per Jabro, that Defendants gather documents pertaining to their financial condition, bring them to trial under seal and make them immediately available in the event that the jury's findings make punitive damages available.

Prevailing party to prepare and submit proposed order to the pro tem judge at tom@thomascohen.com.

Judge Pro Tem: Tom Cohen; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/JPT)
